narrative: As an Asian male applicant, your law school application was evaluated using a predictive model that assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, based on profiles from thousands of past students. The model looked at your academic record, including your undergraduate GPA of 2.70—which is below the typical 3.21 average—your LSAT score of 29 compared to the usual 36.70, your middle family income bracket, full-time student status, and your Year 1 and Year 3 school decile rankings, both at the bottom (1st decile versus averages around 5.5-5.7). It predicted a 71.7% chance that you would fail the bar exam, which is high enough to trigger an automatic rejection to protect the school's outcomes and reputation. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 decile ranking in the lowest group, as lower performance late in school strongly signals challenges ahead on high-stakes exams like the bar. Your Year 1 decile, also at the bottom, reinforced this concern by showing early struggles that didn't improve. On a more positive note, your undergraduate GPA had the third-strongest influence but worked in your favor, pulling the prediction slightly toward passing since it demonstrated some baseline academic capability despite being below average. Compared to typical students who pass, who often rank in the middle or higher deciles and have stronger test scores, your profile aligned more closely with those who faced bar exam difficulties. This isn't a judgment on your potential or background—many from similar family incomes and full-time paths succeed with targeted effort—but the model prioritizes patterns from past data. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score toward the average, strengthening your later-year grades through additional coursework or tutoring, and exploring part-time programs if that fits your situation better. Building a stronger academic trajectory now could shift future predictions in your favor. Schools value persistence, especially for determined applicants like you, so use this as motivation to address those key areas. With focused improvements, reapplying next cycle could yield very different results.
